reason: Closed-book solvability failure: at least one Correct-labeled option (A) requires paper-specific knowledge that separator shutdown at ~125°C is definitively “pore closure” and that this “temporarily reduces electrochemical heat generation.” The stem only states a resistance rise and small OCV drop at 125°C; it does not provide the separator-shutdown mechanism or link to reduced electrochemical heating, so a test-taker without the paper cannot uniquely justify A. Additionally, B and D also hinge on paper-specific causal attribution (self-heating at 175°C being from internal-short Joule heat, and that LFP TR requires >220°C / needs additional heating), which is not stated in the stem.

Closed-book solvability/ambiguity failure: Option B is labeled Correct but relies on an unstated value judgment (“more uniform but elevated thermal risk profile”) and an undefined baseline comparison (risk relative to what metric or operating SoC distribution). From the stem/evidence one can infer ‘more uniform onset temperature’ and ‘lower onset temperature’, but ‘elevated risk profile’ is not uniquely derivable without additional paper- or policy-specific risk definitions. Because B can be reasonably disputed (or reformulated as ‘more uniform but higher risk’) under the same admissible facts, the correct set is not uniquely determined.

MCQ with papers provided. Closed-book solvability fails: options A and F require knowing the specific venting drivers (LFP mainly electrolyte volatilization; LMO mainly gas release) and option C requires knowing the post-runaway cathode damage mechanisms (LFP gas-release-driven vs NCM impact-force-driven). These mechanistic facts are not contained in the stem and are paper-specific, so the labeled correct answers cannot be derived from the admissible information set.

Short-answer item; papers provided. The stem itself supplies the key facts needed to reason closed-book: (1) NCA overcharge to 143% SoC lowers onset to ~65°C versus higher at 100% SoC, and (2) aged NCM/graphite at 70% SoH shifts exothermic onset ~30°C lower independent of SoC. The gold answer’s two mechanisms (overcharge-induced Li deposition lowering onset under overcharged conditions; aging/HSAL-related downward onset shift applying across SoC) are logically derivable from the stem and general reasoning about threshold calibration. It also respects the chemistry boundary by not asserting a specific aged-NCA onset or additivity. No contradictions with the evidence bundle (which explicitly states Li deposition for overcharged NCA and ~30°C SoC-independent shift for aged NCM/graphite).
